%   \input{sections/healing_procedure}

\subsection{Post-Hoc Feasibility Repair and Healed Objective}
\label{sec:healing}

Several solvers---D-Wave BQM, D-Wave CQM, and the Gurobi decomposed
variants---return solutions that are \emph{infeasible} with respect to one or
more constraints of the Binary Plot (BP) formulation.
To provide a meaningful quality comparison on a common, feasible baseline, we
apply a greedy, \emph{one-shot} repair procedure per solution and report the
resulting \emph{healed objective} alongside (or in place of) the raw objective.
The healed objective is always $\leq$ the raw objective, so it provides a
\emph{lower-bound} on the feasible value the solver would have obtained had it
returned a feasible solution.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection{Violation Types}
\label{sec:healing:violtypes}

\paragraph{Study~1 (hybrid benchmark).}
The QUBO/CQM formulation of the BP model enforces an
\emph{at-most-one-crop-per-patch} constraint
($\sum_c Y_{p,c} \leq 1$ for each patch $p$).
All observed violations are of this single type: the solver assigns two crops
$c_1, c_2$ to the same patch $p$ (both indicator variables $Y_{p,c_1}$ and
$Y_{p,c_2}$ are set to 1).

\paragraph{Study~2 and Gurobi decomposed (QPU decomposition benchmark).}
Two constraint types are violated in the decomposed formulation:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{One-crop-per-patch} ($\sum_c Y_{p,c} \leq 1$): identical to
        Study~1---the decomposition assigns multiple crops to a single patch.
  \item \textbf{Food-group coverage} ($\sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} Y_{p,c} \geq 1$
        for each food group $g$): at least one patch must be assigned a
        representative crop of each food group; the decomposed solver fails to
        satisfy one or more of these global coverage constraints.
\end{enumerate}

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection{Repair for One-Crop-per-Patch Violations}
\label{sec:healing:onecrop}

Let $n$ denote the total number of patches, $A_{\text{tot}}$ the total
cultivable area, and $A_p$ the area of patch $p$.
Define the normalised contribution of crop $c$ on patch $p$ as
\begin{equation}
  \delta_{p,c} \;=\; b_c \cdot \frac{A_p}{A_{\text{tot}}} ,
  \label{eq:patch_contrib}
\end{equation}
where $b_c \geq 0$ is the benefit coefficient of crop $c$.

For each violating patch $p$ with assigned crops $\mathcal{C}_p$
($|\mathcal{C}_p| \geq 2$), we \textbf{remove the minimum-benefit crop}:
\begin{equation}
  \text{gain}_p \;=\; \min_{c\,\in\,\mathcal{C}_p}\; \delta_{p,c} .
  \label{eq:onecrop_gain}
\end{equation}
The healed objective after repairing the set $\mathcal{V}$ of violating patches is
\begin{equation}
  \tilde{f} \;=\; f_{\text{raw}} - \sum_{p \in \mathcal{V}} \text{gain}_p .
  \label{eq:healed_onecrop}
\end{equation}
This repair is exact: removing one crop from patch $p$ leaves exactly one
assigned crop, fully restoring feasibility for that patch.

For the \textbf{Gurobi decomposed} variants, $\tilde{f}$ is pre-computed during
the benchmark run and stored as \texttt{healed\_objective} in
\texttt{solver\_comparison\_results\_hard.json}.

For the \textbf{D-Wave BQM/CQM} (Study~1), the repair is applied post-hoc from
the stored $\{Y_{p,c}\}$ dictionary by identifying
$\mathcal{C}_p = \{c : Y_{p,c} \geq 0.5\}$ for each violating patch and
applying \cref{eq:healed_onecrop}.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection{Repair for Food-Group Coverage Violations}
\label{sec:healing:foodgroup}

A food-group coverage constraint
$\sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} Y_{p,c} \geq g_{\min}$
is violated when the number of patches assigned to crops of group~$g$
falls short by $\Delta_g = g_{\min} - \sum_{p} Y_{p,g} > 0$ patches.

The repair reassigns $\Delta_g$ existing patch assignments to a representative
crop of group $g$.  Assuming the representative crop has benefit $b_g \approx 0$
(starchy-staple crops constitute the majority of violated groups), the swap cost
equals the full contribution of the re-assigned patch.
We choose the \textbf{minimum-benefit patch} to minimise the objective loss:
\begin{equation}
  \text{gain}_g \;=\; \Delta_g \cdot
    \min_{p\,\in\,\mathcal{P}_{\text{active}}} \;
    b_{c(p)} \cdot \frac{A_p}{A_{\text{tot}}} ,
  \label{eq:foodgroup_gain}
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{P}_{\text{active}}$ is the set of patches not already assigned
to group $g$, and $c(p)$ is the crop assigned to patch $p$.
The total healed objective when both violation types are present is
\begin{equation}
  \tilde{f} \;=\; f_{\text{raw}}
    - \sum_{p\,\in\,\mathcal{V}} \text{gain}_p
    - \sum_{g\,\in\,\mathcal{G}_{\text{viol}}} \text{gain}_g ,
  \label{eq:healed_full}
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{G}_{\text{viol}}$ denotes the set of food groups with coverage
violations ($\Delta_g > 0$).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection{Interpretation}
\label{sec:healing:interp}

The healed objective $\tilde{f}$ provides a pessimistic (lower-bound) estimate
of the feasible solution quality.
A large gap $f_{\text{raw}} - \tilde{f}$ signals that the solver's objective
inflation is substantially driven by constraint violations: the reported
$f_{\text{raw}}$ is not a valid crop-allocation benefit.
Conversely, a small gap — as observed for most QPU methods and all Gurobi
decomposed variants beyond $n = 25$ farms — indicates that the solution is
\emph{nearly} feasible and only minor repairs are needed.

In the figures, raw objectives are shown as solid lines and healed objectives
as dotted lines of matching colour.  Scales at which raw and healed coincide
(no violations) are omitted from the dotted series.
